entry.title: Venue for SAP Inside Track 2026 Announced!
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce that SAP Inside Track Sydney 2026 will be held at The Mint on Friday, September 4.

As one of Sydney’s most historic and character-filled venues, The Mint brings a unique sense of place to this year’s event.  Its heritage architecture and rich history create a memorable backdrop, while the striking modern auditorium and beautiful surrounds offer the perfect setting for a fresh, elevated SAP Inside Track experience.

Its CBD location on Macquarie Street is highly convenient for attendees, within walking distance of Martin Place, Town Hall and Wynyard.

This combination of history, design, and central city convenience makes The Mint an ideal home for a reimagined SIT Sydney — one that brings the SAP community together in a setting for learning, conversation, connection and inspiration.

🗓️ Date: Friday, September 4, 2026 | 9am to 6pm
📍Location: The Mint, 10 Macquarie Street, Sydney

More details coming soon, including registration, call for speakers, and sponsorship opportunities.

We can’t wait to welcome you at The Mint!

#SAPInsideTrack #SITSydney #SAPCommunity #SAPChampions

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite Licensing: A Practical Guide for Businesses and Architects
entry.content:
Introduction

Within the SAP ecosystem, developers benefit from extensive documentation and technical tutorials. However, business leaders, enterprise architects, procurement teams, and startups often struggle to find clear guidance on licensing, pricing, and commercial models—particularly for SAP Integration Suite.

This blog bridges that gap by offering a practical, business-focused overview of SAP Integration Suite licensing, along with real-world case studies that illustrate how organizations can select the most suitable commercial model.

Why Licensing Information Is Hard to Find

Unlike technical documentation, commercial details are not widely publicized. Key reasons include:

1. Tailored Pricing Models

SAP solutions are customised based on:

  • Organization size and industry
  • Integration volumes and complexity
  • Geographic presence
  • Compliance requirements
  • Existing SAP investments

2. Sales-Led Engagement Model

Licensing information is typically provided through:

  • SAP account executives
  • Authorized SAP partners
  • Commercial proposals and Statements of Work

3. Consumption-Based Cloud Economics

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) follows a consumption-based model where costs depend on usage metrics such as:

  • Integration runtime capacity
  • Messages processed
  • API calls and events
  • Compute and memory utilization
  • Additional capabilities like B2B Integration and API Management

4. Rapid Evolution of SAP Offerings

SAP frequently enhances its cloud offerings, making static pricing references less reliable over time.

Overview of SAP Integration Suite Commercial Models

1. Free Tier

Best For: Developers, students, and innovation teams

Purpose: Learning, experimentation, and prototyping

Benefits:

  • No cost
  • Ideal for proofs of concept
  • Hands-on experience

Limitations:

  • Restricted resources
  • Not intended for production use

2. Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG)

Best For: Startups, SMEs, and organizations with unpredictable workloads

Benefits:

  • No upfront commitment
  • Pay only for what you consume
  • Rapid scalability and flexibility

Considerations:

  • Requires cost governance
  • Expenses increase with scale

3. Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (CPEA)

Best For: Large enterprises adopting multiple SAP BTP services

Benefits:

  • Prepaid cloud credits
  • Flexibility across SAP BTP services
  • Volume-based discounts
  • Centralized governance

Considerations:

  • Requires upfront financial commitment
  • Needs consumption planning

4. Subscription-Based Licensing

Best For: Organizations with stable and predictable workloads

Benefits:

  • Predictable costs
  • Simplified budgeting
  • Guaranteed capacity

Considerations:

  • Less flexibility compared to consumption models
  • Potential risk of underutilization

5. RISE with SAP

Best For: Organizations adopting SAP S/4HANA Cloud as part of digital transformation

Benefits:

  • Bundled services under a single contract
  • Simplified vendor management
  • Accelerated cloud adoption

Considerations:

  • Less granular pricing visibility
  • Best suited for SAP-centric landscapes

Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Model

Business Scenario

Recommended Model

Learning and experimentation

Free Tier

Startups building their first integrations

Pay-As-You-Go

SMEs with growing integration needs

PAYG transitioning to CPEA

Enterprises adopting multiple BTP services

CPEA

Predictable, high-volume workloads

Subscription-Based Licensing

S/4HANA Cloud transformation initiatives

RISE with SAP

System integrators and consulting firms

PAYG for projects; CPEA for internal use

Key Cost Drivers for SAP Integration Suite

Cost Driver

Business Impact

Number of integrations

Influences runtime capacity

Message volume

Major contributor to consumption costs

API calls

Impacts API Management usage

B2B/EDI capabilities

Adds specialized costs

Number of environments (Dev, QA, Prod)

Increases consumption

Data transformation complexity

Affects processing requirements

High availability and scalability

Raises infrastructure costs

Security and compliance requirements

May increase operational overhead

Real-World Case Studies

Case Study 1: Startup Integrating SaaS Applications

Industry: HR Technology Startup

Challenge: Integrate a cloud-based HR platform with SAP S/4HANA for clients.

Solution: Pay-As-You-Go model.

Implementation:

  • Connected Salesforce, SAP S/4HANA, and SuccessFactors.
  • Leveraged API Management and Cloud Integration.
  • Scaled gradually based on customer demand.

Outcome:

  • Zero upfront investment.
  • Faster time-to-market.
  • Controlled operational expenses.

Why PAYG Was Ideal:

  • Unpredictable workloads.
  • Need for agility and cost efficiency.

Case Study 2: Manufacturing Enterprise Modernizing Legacy Systems

Industry: Global Manufacturing

Challenge: Replace point-to-point integrations across SAP ECC, Ariba, and third-party logistics providers.

Solution: Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (CPEA).

Implementation:

  • Standardized enterprise integrations using SAP Integration Suite.
  • Enabled EDI and API-based partner connectivity.
  • Utilized BTP credits across multiple services.

Outcome:

  • Reduced integration complexity.
  • Improved supply chain visibility.
  • Optimized costs through prepaid credits.

Why CPEA Was Ideal:

  • Large-scale, enterprise-wide adoption.
  • Need for flexibility across multiple BTP services.

Case Study 3: Retail Chain Managing Seasonal Demand

Industry: Retail and E-Commerce

Challenge: Handle fluctuating integration volumes during festive and seasonal peaks.

Solution: Pay-As-You-Go model.

Implementation:

  • Integrated SAP Commerce Cloud with logistics and payment gateways.
  • Scaled integrations during peak sales periods.

Outcome:

  • Cost optimization during off-peak seasons.
  • Elastic scalability during high demand.

Why PAYG Was Ideal:

  • Variable workloads and seasonal spikes.

Case Study 4: Banking Institution Requiring Predictable Costs

Industry: Banking and Financial Services

Challenge: Ensure secure, compliant, and stable integrations across core banking systems.

Solution: Subscription-Based Licensing.

Implementation:

  • Integrated SAP and non-SAP systems with strict governance.
  • Ensured regulatory compliance and high availability.

Outcome:

  • Predictable budgeting and financial planning.
  • Stable, mission-critical operations.

Why Subscription Was Ideal:

  • Consistent transaction volumes.
  • Strong compliance requirements.

Case Study 5: Enterprise Migrating to SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Industry: Professional Services

Challenge: Transform legacy systems while migrating to SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Solution: RISE with SAP.

Implementation:

  • Leveraged pre-packaged integrations and clean-core principles.
  • Enabled seamless connectivity across cloud applications.

Outcome:

  • Simplified vendor and contract management.
  • Accelerated digital transformation.

Why RISE with SAP Was Ideal:

  • Unified approach to ERP modernization and integration.

A Typical Adoption Journey

Most organizations follow this path:

Free Tier → Pay-As-You-Go → CPEA or Subscription → RISE with SAP

This approach enables businesses to:

  • Validate use cases
  • Optimize investments
  • Scale efficiently
  • Reduce financial risks

Essential Questions to Ask SAP or Partners

Before selecting a commercial model, consider asking:

  1. What metrics determine Integration Suite costs?
  2. Which capabilities are included in the license?
  3. How are non-production environments billed?
  4. Are there volume discounts available?
  5. What governance tools exist for cost monitoring?
  6. Can the commercial model be converted later?
  7. What is the estimated total cost of ownership over three years?
  8. How does pricing scale as the organization grows?

Recommended Resources

  • SAP Discovery Center 
  • SAP BTP Pricing Calculator
  • SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Community Blogs and Q&A
  • SAP Learning Hub and openSAP
  • Authorized SAP Partners and Account Executives

Key Takeaways

  • Pay-As-You-Go is a licensing model—not a license-free option.
  • CPEA provides flexibility and cost optimization for enterprises.
  • Subscription models offer predictable costs for stable workloads.
  • RISE with SAP simplifies ERP-led transformations.
  • Architects must align technical architecture with commercial strategy.
  • Understanding licensing early prevents costly redesigns later.

Conclusion

While developers benefit from extensive technical guidance, businesses and architects often lack clarity around licensing and pricing. By understanding SAP Integration Suite’s commercial models, organizations can make informed decisions that align technology investments with business goals.

Selecting the right licensing model ensures scalability, cost efficiency, and long-term success in the cloud integration journey.

Call to Action

Have you implemented SAP Integration Suite using PAYG, CPEA, Subscription, or RISE with SAP? Share your experiences and insights in the comments to help the SAP Community learn and grow together.

Thank you for reading!

Connect in LinkedIn : Naveen Kumar 

Disclaimer:
This article reflects personal insights and is intended for educational and informational purposes only. It does not represent the official position of SAP. Pricing, licensing, and commercial models are subject to change and may vary by region and customer agreement. For the most accurate and up-to-date information, please consult SAP directly or an authorized SAP partner. SAP and other SAP products mentioned herein are trademarks or registered trademarks of SAP SE in Germany and other countries.

entry.title: A memorable SAP Community gathering at the SAP Inside Track Campinas 2026
entry.content:
The SAP Inside Track Campinas 2026, the SIT edition number 60th in Brazil, was truly a remarkable event bringing together an impressive lineup of speakers and community members. It marked a special moment for the SAP ecosystem in Brazil, especially with the first visit of SAP Developer Advocates to the country. 

We had the honor of welcoming @noravonthenen @thomas_jung @RichHeilman @Vitaliy-R and SAP Community managers @antonia_zorn @josh_bentley, although Antonia is based in Brazil for some time, originally comes from Germany. Their presence, along with the broader SAP Community in Brazil, made this event in Campinas even more meaningful.

This visit was part of an extensive SAP CodeJam Roadshow across Brazil. For the first time, the country hosted more than 17 CodeJam sessions, hands-on training events focused on SAP development. It was an impressive milestone, and you can check the full events agenda in Brazil through this link (SIT + Codejam + SAP Meet ups).

The event itself took place at FACAMP, a beautiful university campus surrounded by green areas and open spaces. The day started with a warm welcome coffee break, followed by a full agenda that included lunch and additional networking breaks.

The opening session featured Josh and Antonia Zorn the SAP Community, followed by Thomas Jung and Rich, who spoke about ABAP AI topics that have been gaining strong traction in recent CodeJams. The agenda continued with several high-quality sessions from speakers like Vitaliy, Nora, @Secco , Leandro Dante, @MRobalinho among others. I’ve included links below so you can watch all sessions on YouTube.

 

Session Title Speaker(s)

ABAP AI — Artificial Intelligence in ABAP DevelopmentSAP Community managers @antonia_zorn @josh_bentley
SAP Developer Advocates @thomas_jung @RichHeilman
Tax Reform — 2026 RoadmapRoberto Paiva
GenAI — A Practical View of Generative AI in the SAP Ecosystem@noravonthenen
AI in SAP HANA Cloud@Vitaliy-R
Automatic PIX Implementation@MRobalinho 
AI, Geopolitics, and the IT Bubble — The SAP Architecture Market OutlookMarcelo Figueiredo Cabrera
From Data to Decisions — Accelerating Results with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)Renata Cristina
Transformation Focused on Business Value GenerationRodrigo Cordeiro
SAP BTP in Practice — Integrating Solutions and Connecting SAP ProductsDiego Yegros
SAP BTP Multitenancy in Practice — From Add-On to the FutureJoão Luiz Martins
GROW Retail Fashion — Architecture, Scalability, and Changes in S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition)Diego Bortolucci
LLM, Joule, and RPT-1 — The New AI Stack for Structured Data in SAPLeandro Dante
AI for Functional Consultants — From Basic GPT to Prototyping@Secco 
Giveaways & Raffles — Closing SessionOrganization

One of the highlights of the event was its relaxed and friendly atmosphere. Attendees felt comfortable engaging with speakers, taking photos, and networking throughout the day. Unlike many events where people attend only a few sessions, most participants stayed until the very end, clear evidence of the quality and engagement of the content.

For me personally, it was also a special experience. I had the opportunity to contribute as a volunteer, supporting the live streaming of the event. While that meant I couldn’t take as many photos as I would have liked, it was incredibly rewarding to reconnect with friends and meet so many great professionals from the SAP community. 

Campinas continues to be a key hub for SAP events, attracting not only local participants but also many attendees from São Paulo. Alongside other cities like Ribeirão Preto and São José dos Campos, it plays an important role in strengthening the SAP ecosystem in the region.

 If you are an SAP professional, I strongly recommend keeping an eye on upcoming SAP Inside Track events in your area. Follow the SAP Community São Paulo group on LinkedIn and stay connected, you don’t want to miss these opportunities for learning, networking, and reconnecting with amazing people in the SAP world.

Special thanks to @RobertaFernande and the organizing team for making this event possible.

Feel free to share your own photos and experiences, or check out the SAP Inside Track LinkedIn page for more highlights from the event. The level of community engagement and social media activity around this event was truly outstanding.

It was a pleasure to be part of SAP Inside Track Campinas 2026. Looking forward to seeing you all again at future events.

entry.title: Introduction- Abhishek
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
 
I’ve been following SAP Community content for a while, and finally decided to become more active here.
 
I currently work as a Solutions Architect focusing on SAP BTP and S/4HANA transformation programs. Most of my work revolves around designing extension architectures, managing multi-subaccount landscapes, and setting up governance models.
 
I’m particularly interested in topics like:
  • Subaccount strategy and landscape design
  • Identity and access management across BTP
  • Transport and deployment governance (CTMS, DevOps patterns)
  • AI use cases
  • Clean-core aligned extension approaches
I’m here to learn from others’ experiences and also share some of the patterns and lessons I’ve seen in production environments.
 
Looking forward to connecting and contributing.

entry.title: SAP Test Automation PM from Korea — Excited to Join the Community
entry.content:
Hi everyone!

I'm Arum, a Product Manager at LG CNS in Seoul, South Korea, working on SAP test automation.

My focus areas include S/4HANA migration testing strategies, Cloud ALM transitions, and regression testing at scale in large ERP environments.

I joined this community to exchange ideas with fellow SAP practitioners — especially around test automation approaches, SolMan to Cloud ALM transition challenges, and how to run testing efficiently during ERP transformation projects.

Looking forward to learning from all of you!

entry.title: CodeSage for SAP - (Building an AI-Powered ABAP Knowledge Platform from Scratch)
entry.content:
What is CodeSage for SAP:

The most prominent technical use of "CodeSage" is for a series of open-source embedding models developed for software engineering tasks like code search and representation learning. Using the same concept wants to create a code knowledge assistant for ABAP development teams. It gives every developer — new or experienced — the ability to query your organization's entire codebase in plain English and receive accurate, contextualized answers grounded in your own source code.

Who will build the CodeSage for SAP:

This artifact will be very useful for multiple purposes, such as learning and project delivery etc. It could be built as a project by institutions, large orgs running on SAP.  This is trained on specific ABAP landscape: naming conventions, Codes, error classes, enhancement patterns, clean core approach, SAP best practice etc.

How to build CodeSage for SAP:

Phase

What It Builds

Runs Where

Phase 1

Extracts ABAP source from SAP via RFC

Windows PC

Phase 2

Embeds code into ChromaDB vector index

Windows PC (CPU)

Phase 3

Fine-tunes LLaMA-3 with QLoRA, deploys to BYOM

Kaggle (free GPU)

Phase 4

Deploys CAP agent on BTP for developer queries

SAP BTP

 PHASE 1  —  Scan & Extract

Phase 1 reads your SAP system and saves every custom ABAP object (Z*/Y* namespace) as plain text files on your local Windows machine. The output is a folder of .abap source files and .json metadata sidecars that feed both Phase 2 and Phase 3.

Think of it as taking a complete photograph of your codebase at a point in time. The extractor uses pyrfc — a Python library that speaks SAP's RFC protocol — to call standard SAP function modules (RPY_PROGRAM_READ, RPY_FUNCTIONMODULE_READ) and pull source code line by line. A credential filter strips any hard-coded passwords before files are written to disk.

Artifacts:

Folder Structure

scan_config.yaml — controls what gets extracted

sap:
  host:     "your-sap-host"
  sysnr:    "00"
  client:   "100"
  user:     "CODESAGE_RFC"
  password: "${SAP_RFC_PASSWORD}"    # Never store passwords in plain text

output_dir: "./abap_files"

namespaces:
  - "Z*"
  - "Y*"

object_types:
  - PROG    # Programs / Reports
  - FUGR    # Function Groups (contains Function Modules)
  - CLAS    # ABAP Classes
  - INTF    # Interfaces
  - ENHO    # BAdI Implementations

exclude_packages:
  - "$TMP"  # Skip local dev objects

extractor.py — RFC extraction script

import pyrfc, json, pathlib, yaml, re

cfg = yaml.safe_load(open('scan_config.yaml'))
out = pathlib.Path(cfg['output_dir'])
out.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

# Strip any hard-coded credentials before saving to disk
CRED_RE = re.compile(
r'(password|passwd|pwd|secret|apikey)\s*=\s*[\'\"][^\'\"]+[\'\"]', re.I)

conn = pyrfc.Connection(
ashost=cfg['sap']['host'], sysnr=cfg['sap']['sysnr'],
client=cfg['sap']['client'], user=cfg['sap']['user'],
passwd=cfg['sap']['password'])

def save_object(name, obj_type, source, meta):
source_clean = CRED_RE.sub('[REDACTED]', source)
(out / f'{name}.abap').write_text(source_clean, encoding='utf-8')
(out / f'{name}.json').write_text(json.dumps(meta, indent=2), encoding='utf-8')

# Extract Programs
progs = conn.call('RPY_DIRECTORY_FINISH', OBJECT_TYPE='PROG', GENERIC_NAME='Z*')
for obj in progs['TADIR']:
try:
r = conn.call('RPY_PROGRAM_READ', PROG_NAME=obj['OBJ_NAME'])
src='\n'.join(l['LINE'] for l in r['SOURCE'])
save_object(obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'PROG', src, {
'name': obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'type': 'PROG',
'package': obj.get('DEVCLASS',''), 'changed': obj.get('LDATE','')})
except Exception as e:
print(f' SKIP {obj["OBJ_NAME"]}: {e}')

# Extract Function Modules
fugs = conn.call('RPY_DIRECTORY_FINISH', OBJECT_TYPE='FUGR', GENERIC_NAME='Z*')
for obj in fugs['TADIR']:
try:
r = conn.call('RPY_FUNCTIONMODULE_READ', FUNCNAME=obj['OBJ_NAME'])
src='\n'.join(l['LINE'] for l in r.get('SOURCE',[]))
save_object(obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'FUGR', src, {
'name': obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'type': 'FM',
'package': obj.get('DEVCLASS',''), 'changed': obj.get('LDATE','')})
except Exception as e:
print(f' SKIP {obj["OBJ_NAME"]}: {e}')

print(f'Done. {len(list(out.glob("*.abap")))} objects saved to {out}/')

Prerequisites & How to Run

Step 1 — Install SAP NW RFC SDK

# 1. Download "SAP NW RFC SDK 7.50 Windows 64-bit" from SAP Support Portal (Note 2573790)
# 2. Extract to C:\nwrfcsdk\  (you should see C:\nwrfcsdk\lib\sapnwrfc.dll)
# 3. Add to Windows PATH:
#    Win+S -> "Edit the system environment variables" -> Environment Variables
#    -> System Variables -> Path -> Edit -> New -> C:\nwrfcsdk\lib -> OK
# 4. Verify in a NEW PowerShell window:
Get-Item "C:\nwrfcsdk\lib\sapnwrfc.dll"   # Should list the file

Step 2 — Create virtual environment and install dependencies

cd C:\codesage\phase1_extract

python -m venv codesage-env
.\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1
# If blocked by execution policy, run this first:
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

pip install pyrfc pyyaml
python -c "import pyrfc; print('pyrfc OK')"
python -c "import yaml;  print('pyyaml OK')"

Step 3 — Store SAP password as environment variable

# Current session only (recommended for dev/test):
$env:SAP_RFC_PASSWORD = "your_sap_password_here"

# Persist across reboots (stored in Windows registry):
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
    "SAP_RFC_PASSWORD", "your_sap_password_here", "User")

Step 4 — Test RFC connection

# Save as test_connection.py, then run: python test_connection.py
import pyrfc, os, yaml
cfg  = yaml.safe_load(open('scan_config.yaml'))
conn = pyrfc.Connection(
    ashost=cfg['sap']['host'],   sysnr=cfg['sap']['sysnr'],
    client=cfg['sap']['client'], user=cfg['sap']['user'],
    passwd=os.environ['SAP_RFC_PASSWORD'])
result = conn.call('RFC_PING')
print('RFC connection successful:', result)
# Expected:  RFC connection successful: {}

python test_connection.py

Step 5 — Run the extractor

cd C:\codesage\phase1_extract
python extractor.py
# Console shows: SKIP messages for deleted/inaccessible objects (normal)
# Finishes with: Done. 8,432 objects saved to .\abap_files\

Step 6 — Verify output

# Count extracted files
(Get-ChildItem -Path ".\abap_files" -Filter "*.abap").Count
# Typical: 5,000 to 20,000

# Confirm no plain-text passwords in output
Select-String -Path ".\abap_files\*.abap" -Pattern "password" -CaseSensitive:$false |
  Where-Object { $_.Line -notmatch "REDACTED" }
# If nothing returned: credential filter is working correctly

Step 7 — Schedule monthly re-run (optional)

$action  = New-ScheduledTaskAction `
    -Execute "C:\codesage\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\python.exe" `
    -Argument "C:\codesage\phase1_extract\extractor.py" `
    -WorkingDirectory "C:\codesage\phase1_extract"
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Monthly -DaysOfMonth 1 -At "02:00AM"
Register-ScheduledTask `
    -TaskName "CodeSage Phase1 Monthly Extract" `
    -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -RunLevel Highest

Phase1 Outcome:  A local folder (abap_files/) containing .abap source and .json metadata for every custom object in your SAP system (Z*/Y* namespace). Credentials are filtered, the RFC connection is read-only, and no data leaves your network. This folder is the shared input for Phase 2 (vector indexing) and Phase 3 (fine-tuning).

PHASE 2  —  Index & Embed

Phase 2 transforms the raw ABAP source files from Phase 1 into a searchable semantic vector database. 

Two steps run in sequence:

  • Step 1 — chunker.py:  Chunking: splits each ABAP file at logical boundaries (FORM/ENDFORM, METHOD/ENDMETHOD). One chunk = one function module, one method, or one report section. 150–500 tokens per chunk.
  • Step 2 — embedder.py:  Embedding: converts each chunk to a 768-number vector using the nomic-embed-text model running locally via Ollama. Stores vectors + original text in ChromaDB (4 collections).

The four ChromaDB collections:

Collection

Content

Chunk Unit

abap_programs

Executable reports and includes

One FORM routine or report body

function_modules

Function module source + parameters

One complete function module

abap_classes

ABAP OO classes, interfaces, methods

One METHOD block

documentation

SAP Help, Clean Core guidelines (optional)

One documentation paragraph

 Artifacts:

Folder Structure

chunker.py — split ABAP source into logical segments

import pathlib, json, re
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Chunk:
    text: str; object_name: str; chunk_type: str; collection: str

FORM_RE      = re.compile(r'^FORM\s+(\w+)', re.M | re.I)
METHOD_RE    = re.compile(r'^\s*METHOD\s+(\w+)', re.M | re.I)
ENDFORM_RE   = re.compile(r'^ENDFORM', re.M | re.I)
ENDMETHOD_RE = re.compile(r'^\s*ENDMETHOD', re.M | re.I)

def chunk_abap(abap_path, meta):
    src=abap_path.read_text(encoding='utf-8')
    lines = src.splitlines()
    name  = meta['name'];  typ = meta['type']
    chunks = []

    if typ == 'FM':                             # Function module = one chunk
        chunks.append(Chunk(src, name, 'FUNCTION', 'function_modules'))

    elif typ == 'CLAS':                         # Class = split on METHOD/ENDMETHOD
        buf, mname = [], None
        for line in lines:
            m = METHOD_RE.match(line)
            if m:
                mname = m.group(1); buf = [line]
            elif ENDMETHOD_RE.match(line) and mname:
                buf.append(line)
                chunks.append(Chunk('\n'.join(buf), f'{name}.{mname}',
                                    'METHOD', 'abap_classes'))
                mname = None; buf = []
            elif mname:
                buf.append(line)
        if not chunks:
            chunks.append(Chunk(src, name, 'CLASS_DEF', 'abap_classes'))

    else:                                       # Program = split on FORM/ENDFORM
        buf, fname = [], None
        for line in lines:
            m = FORM_RE.match(line)
            if m:
                fname = m.group(1); buf = [line]
            elif ENDFORM_RE.match(line) and fname:
                buf.append(line)
                chunks.append(Chunk('\n'.join(buf), f'{name}.{fname}',
                                    'FORM', 'abap_programs'))
                fname = None; buf = []
            elif fname:
                buf.append(line)
        chunks.append(Chunk(src[:3000], name, 'BODY', 'abap_programs'))

    return [c for c in chunks if len(c.text.strip()) > 50]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    abap_dir = pathlib.Path('../phase1_extract/abap_files')
    all_chunks = []
    for abap_file in abap_dir.glob('*.abap'):
        meta_file = abap_file.with_suffix('.json')
        meta = json.loads(meta_file.read_text()) if meta_file.exists()                else {'name': abap_file.stem, 'type': 'PROG'}
        all_chunks.extend(chunk_abap(abap_file, meta))
    print(f'Total chunks: {len(all_chunks)}')

embedder.py — embed chunks and store in ChromaDB

import chromadb, requests, json, pathlib
from chunker import chunk_abap

db = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chromadb_store')

COLLECTIONS = {
    'abap_programs':    db.get_or_create_collection('abap_programs'),
    'function_modules': db.get_or_create_collection('function_modules'),
    'abap_classes':     db.get_or_create_collection('abap_classes'),
    'documentation':    db.get_or_create_collection('documentation'),
}

def embed(text):
    """Call local nomic-embed-text via Ollama. No cloud, no API key."""
    r = requests.post('http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings',
                      json={'model': 'nomic-embed-text', 'prompt': text})
    return r.json()['embedding']           # 768-dimensional vector

def store_chunk(chunk, idx):
    col = COLLECTIONS[chunk.collection]
    vec = embed(chunk.text[:2000])
    col.add(
        ids=[f'{chunk.object_name}_{idx}'],
        embeddings=[vec],
        documents=[chunk.text],
        metadatas=[{'object': chunk.object_name, 'type': chunk.chunk_type}])

abap_dir = pathlib.Path('../phase1_extract/abap_files')
for i, abap_file in enumerate(sorted(abap_dir.glob('*.abap'))):
    meta_file = abap_file.with_suffix('.json')
    meta = json.loads(meta_file.read_text()) if meta_file.exists()            else {'name': abap_file.stem, 'type': 'PROG'}
    for j, chunk in enumerate(chunk_abap(abap_file, meta)):
        store_chunk(chunk, j)
    if i % 100 == 0:
        print(f'  Indexed {i} files...')

print('ChromaDB indexing complete.')
for name, col in COLLECTIONS.items():
    print(f'  {name}: {col.count()} chunks')

Prerequisites & How to Run 

Step 1 — Activate venv and install dependencies

cd C:\codesage\phase2_index
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install chromadb requests
python -c "import chromadb; print('chromadb', chromadb.__version__)"

Step 2 — Install Ollama and download embedding model

# Install Ollama from ollama.com/download (Windows installer, free)
# After install, open a NEW PowerShell window:
ollama pull nomic-embed-text

ollama list
# NAME                    SIZE
# nomic-embed-text:latest 274 MB

Step 3 — Test Ollama is responding

# Save as test_ollama.py, run: python test_ollama.py
import requests
r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings",
                  json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": "hello SAP"})
vec = r.json()["embedding"]
print(f"Embedding OK: {len(vec)} dimensions")
# Expected: Embedding OK: 768 dimensions

python test_ollama.py

Step 4 — Dry-run chunker to preview chunk count

python chunker.py
# Expected: Total chunks: 42,318  (varies by codebase size)

Step 5 — Run the embedder (main Phase 2 step, ~2 hours on CPU)

python embedder.py
#   Indexed 0 files...  Indexed 100 files...  ...
# ChromaDB indexing complete.
#   abap_programs:    18,420 chunks
#   function_modules: 12,305 chunks
#   abap_classes:      9,841 chunks

Step 6 — Test semantic search

# Save as test_search.py, run: python test_search.py
import chromadb, requests

def embed(text):
    r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings",
                      json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": text})
    return r.json()["embedding"]

db  = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chromadb_store")
col = db.get_collection("function_modules")
results = col.query(
    query_embeddings=[embed("validate vendor payment terms")],
    n_results=3)
for doc_text, meta in zip(results["documents"][0], results["metadatas"][0]):
    print(f"[{meta['object']}]  {doc_text[:80]}...")
# Expected top result: [Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS]  FUNCTION Z_VALIDATE...

python test_search.py

Phase2 Outcome:  ChromaDB running on-premise with 4 collections and tens of thousands of embedded ABAP chunks. Any developer query can be matched to the most relevant code in your system in under 100 ms — without sending a single line of code to the cloud. This vector index is used at runtime (Phase 4) to feed context into every LLM response. 

PHASE 3  —  Fine-Tune, Merge & Package for BYOM

Phase 3 is the heart of CodeSage. By the end of it, you have a version of LLaMA-3 that has learned your naming conventions, your preferred BAPIs, your error class patterns, and your ABAP coding style.

Artifacts:

Folder Structure

generate_qa.py — Step A: create training dataset via Claude

import anthropic, json, pathlib

client = anthropic.Anthropic()     # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var

def generate_qa(chunk_text, object_name):
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"You are an ABAP expert. Given this code from {object_name}, "
            f"write 4 developer questions whose answer is in this code.\n\n"
            f"CODE:\n{chunk_text}\n\nReturn a JSON list of strings."}])
    questions = json.loads(msg.content[0].text)
    return [{"prompt": q, "completion": chunk_text} for q in questions]

with open("training_data.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for path in pathlib.Path("../phase1_extract/abap_files").glob("*.abap"):
        chunk = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")[:2000]
        for pair in generate_qa(chunk, path.stem):
            f.write(json.dumps(pair) + "\n")
# Result: 30,000 - 50,000 lines in training_data.jsonl

train_qlora.py — Step B: QLoRA fine-tuning 

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from trl import SFTTrainer
import torch

MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"

# Load base model in 4-bit NF4 (reduces GPU memory from ~28 GB to ~6-12 GB)
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True)

model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID,
              quantization_config=bnb_config, device_map="auto")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)

# Attach LoRA adapters — only these tiny matrices will be trained
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16, lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none",
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Output: trainable params: 13,631,488 (0.17% of 8B)

# Train
trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    dataset_text_field="text",
    max_seq_length=2048,
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./lora-adapter",
        num_train_epochs=3,
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        fp16=True, save_strategy="epoch", logging_steps=50))
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./lora-adapter")   # ~50 MB adapter file

merge_model.py — Step C: merge LoRA adapter into base model

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from peft import PeftModel

base      = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    torch_dtype="auto", device_map="cpu")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")

model  = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./lora-adapter/")
merged = model.merge_and_unload()        # adapter deltas baked into base weights

merged.save_pretrained("./merged_model/")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./merged_model/")
print("Merge complete. ./merged_model/ (~16 GB) ready for Docker packaging.")

Dockerfile — Step 'D: vLLM container for SAP AI Core BYOM

FROM vllm/vllm-openai:latest
COPY ./merged_model/ /model/
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["python", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server", \
     "--model",             "/model", \
     "--port",              "8080", \
     "--served-model-name", "codesage-llama3", \
     "--max-model-len",     "8192", \
     "--dtype",             "bfloat16"]

serving-template.yaml — Step E: SAP AI Core BYOM registration

apiVersion: ai.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: ServingTemplate
metadata:
  name: codesage-llama3
  labels:
    scenarios.ai.sap.com/id: codesage-scenario
    ai.sap.com/version: "1.0"
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
        - name: <your-registry-secret>
      containers:
        - name: kserve-container
          image: <your-registry>/codesage-llama3:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "20Gi"
            limits:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"    # SAP AI Core allocates a managed GPU

Prerequisites & How to Run

Step A — Generate training data 

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install anthropic
$env:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = "sk-ant-your-key-here"

python generate_qa.py
# Reads each .abap file, sends to Claude, saves (question, code) pairs

(Get-Content .\training_data.jsonl).Count
# Typical: 30,000 to 50,000 lines

Step B — Fine-tune: choose your GPU option

Option

GPU

Cost

RAM

Duration

Best For

Option A — Kaggle (recommended for first run)

NVIDIA T4 16 GB (free)

£0

16 GB GPU + 13 GB RAM

6–12 hrs

Anyone, no hardware needed

Option B — Local NVIDIA GPU

RTX 3090 (24 GB) / RTX 4090 (24 GB) / A100 (40–80 GB)

Electricity only

24+ GB VRAM recommended

2–6 hrs

Faster iteration, larger batch, keep data on-prem

 Option A — Kaggle free GPU (T4 16 GB)

  1. Upload training_data.jsonl to Kaggle as a Dataset (kaggle.com → Datasets → New Dataset).
  2. Create a new Kaggle Notebook. In the right panel set Accelerator = GPU T4 x1 and enable Internet access.
  3. In the first notebook cell, install dependencies:

# Kaggle Cell 1 — install + HuggingFace login
!pip install -q transformers peft trl bitsandbytes accelerate datasets huggingface_hub
import huggingface_hub
huggingface_hub.login(token="hf_YOUR_READ_TOKEN")

  1. Paste the contents of train_qlora.py into Cell 2 (update the dataset path to your Kaggle input path).
  2. Click Run All. Training takes 6–12 hours. You can close the browser — Kaggle keeps running.
  3. When complete, download the lora-adapter/ folder as ZIP. Extract to C:\codesage\phase3_finetune\lora-adapter\

Option B — Local NVIDIA GPU (RTX 3090 / 4090 / A100)

If you have a Windows machine with a modern NVIDIA GPU you can run the full training pipeline locally. This keeps all training data on-premise (no upload to Kaggle) and is typically 2–3× faster than a T4 due to higher memory bandwidth.

Additional prerequisites for local GPU training:

Step B2.1 — Verify NVIDIA setup in PowerShell

# Check GPU is recognised and driver version
nvidia-smi
# Expected output includes: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 / 4090 / A100
#   Driver Version: 535.xx  |  CUDA Version: 12.x

# Check CUDA toolkit
nvcc --version
# Expected: Cuda compilation tools, release 11.8 (or 12.x)

# If nvcc not found, CUDA Toolkit is not installed.
# Download from: https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads
# Choose: Windows -> x86_64 -> 11 -> exe (local)

Step B2.2 — Install Python dependencies for local GPU training

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Install PyTorch with CUDA support (match your CUDA version)
# For CUDA 11.8:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
# For CUDA 12.1:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

# Verify GPU is visible to PyTorch
python -c "import torch; print('CUDA available:', torch.cuda.is_available()); print('GPU:', torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))"
# Expected: CUDA available: True  |  GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090

# Install training libraries
pip install transformers peft trl accelerate datasets huggingface_hub

# Install bitsandbytes (4-bit quantisation for QLoRA)
pip install bitsandbytes
# If bitsandbytes gives DLL errors on Windows, use the community Windows build:
pip install bitsandbytes --prefer-binary --extra-index-url=https://jllllll.github.io/bitsandbytes-windows-webui

Step B2.3 — Download LLaMA-3 base model weights

# Log in to HuggingFace (you must have accepted the Meta LLaMA-3 licence first)
# Visit: https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct and click Accept
python -c "import huggingface_hub; huggingface_hub.login(token='hf_YOUR_READ_TOKEN')"

# Download model (~16 GB, one-time)
python -c "
from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download
snapshot_download('meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct',
                  local_dir='C:\\codesage\\phase3_finetune\\base_model')
print('Download complete.')
"

# Check folder size (should be ~16 GB)
(Get-ChildItem -Path ".\base_model" -Recurse | Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum / 1GB

Step B2.4 — Update train_qlora.py to use the local model path

The only change needed from the Kaggle version is the MODEL_ID path — point it to the local download instead of HuggingFace Hub:

# In train_qlora.py, change this line:
MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"     # Kaggle version (downloads from HF)

# To this for local GPU:
MODEL_ID = "C:\\codesage\\phase3_finetune\\base_model"   # uses local download

# You can also increase batch size on a 24 GB GPU for faster training:
# In TrainingArguments, change:
per_device_train_batch_size=4   # T4 safe value
# To:
per_device_train_batch_size=8   # RTX 3090/4090 with 24 GB VRAM

Step B2.5 — Run training locally

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Monitor GPU memory before starting
nvidia-smi

# Run training
python train_qlora.py

# In a second PowerShell window, monitor GPU usage while training runs:
while ($true) { nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,memory.used,memory.total --format=csv,noheader; Start-Sleep 10 }
# Expected: gpu-util 95-100%,  memory.used ~14000 MiB / 24576 MiB (RTX 3090)

# Training completes in 2-6 hours (vs 6-12 on Kaggle T4)
# Adapter saved to: .\lora-adapter\

Step C — Merge adapter on Windows PC (~20 min, needs 32 GB RAM)

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install transformers peft accelerate
python merge_model.py

# Verify output
Get-ChildItem .\merged_model
# Expected: config.json, tokenizer.json, model-00001-of-00004.safetensors, etc.

Step D — Build and push Docker image (Docker Desktop required)

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune

docker login   # enter Docker Hub credentials
docker build -t codesage-llama3:v1 .    # ~18 GB image, takes 10-20 min first time

docker tag  codesage-llama3:v1  your-dockerhub-username/codesage-llama3:v1
docker push your-dockerhub-username/codesage-llama3:v1  # 30-60 min upload

Step E — Deploy to SAP AI Core (browser, SAP AI Launchpad)

  1. Open SAP AI Launchpad → ML Operations → Docker Registry Secrets → Add (your registry + credentials).
  2. ML Operations → Serving Templates → Upload YAML → select serving-template.yaml.
  3. ML Operations → Configurations → Create → select the codesage-llama3 template.
  4. ML Operations → Deployments → Create → select the configuration. Wait for status: RUNNING (5–10 min).
  5. Copy the Deployment URL and Deployment ID. Store both in BTP → Connectivity → Destinations as destination CODESAGE_AICORE.

Test the endpoint from PowerShell:

# test_aicore.py  -- python test_aicore.py
import requests
token_resp = requests.post("https://<token-url>/oauth/token",
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
    auth=("<client-id>", "<client-secret>"))
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

response = requests.post(
    "https://<ai-core-url>/v2/inference/deployments/<deployment-id>/chat/completions",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "AI-Resource-Group": "default"},
    json={"model": "codesage-llama3",
          "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is ABAP?"}],
          "max_tokens": 200})
print(response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])
# Expected: ABAP stands for Advanced Business Application Programming...

Phase 3 Outcome:  A fine-tuned LLaMA-3 8B model — trained on your organisation's ABAP codebase using QLoRA — is running as a live, OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint on SAP AI Core. No local GPU is needed at runtime. Training cost: £0 on Kaggle free tier. The endpoint is reachable from BTP via a secure Destination. Phases 1, 2, and 3 are now all complete — Phase 4 wires them together.

PHASE 4  —  Runtime Query via SAP BTP

Phase 4 is the user-facing layer. It deploys a small Node.js service (BTP CAP) called the CodeSage Agent that orchestrates the full query pipeline:

Step

What Happens

Where

1

Developer types a plain English question in the UI or Teams bot

Browser / Teams

2

Agent embeds the question using nomic-embed-text (local Ollama)

On-premise

3

ChromaDB returns the top-5 most relevant ABAP code chunks

On-premise

4

Agent builds a structured prompt: context chunks + question

BTP CAP service

5

Prompt is sent to the fine-tuned LLaMA-3 on SAP AI Core BYOM

SAP AI Core

6

Answer returned in 2–5 s, citing real program names by name

Browser / Teams

 Artifacts:

Folder Structure

codesage-service.cds — service endpoint definition

// srv/codesage-service.cds
service CodeSageService @(requires: 'CodeSage.User') {

    // POST /codesage/query  { question, context_filter? }
    action query(
        question       : String not null,
        context_filter : String    // optional: 'function_modules' | 'abap_classes' etc.
    ) returns {
        answer      : String;
        sources     : array of { object_name: String; chunk_type: String; };
        model       : String;
        latency_ms  : Integer;
    };
}

query-handler.js — core orchestration: RAG retrieval + AI Core call

// srv/query-handler.js
const axios  = require('axios');
const xsenv  = require('@sap/xsenv');
const { retrieveChunks } = require('./chromadb-client');

const { destination } = xsenv.getServices({ destination: { tag: 'destination' } });

module.exports = (srv) => {
  srv.on('query', async (req) => {
    const { question, context_filter } = req.data;
    const t0 = Date.now();

    // 1. Retrieve top-5 relevant ABAP chunks from on-premise ChromaDB
    const collections = context_filter
      ? [context_filter]
      : ['abap_programs', 'function_modules', 'abap_classes'];
    const chunks  = await retrieveChunks(question, collections, 5);
    const context = chunks
      .map((c, i) => `[${i+1}] ${c.metadata.object} (${c.metadata.type}):\n${c.document}`)
      .join('\n\n');

    // 2. Build prompt
    const messages = [
      { role: 'system',
        content: 'You are CodeSage, an expert ABAP assistant. '
               + 'Answer ONLY using the retrieved context. '
               + 'Cite every object by name. End with a Sources section.' },
      { role: 'user',
        content: `RETRIEVED CONTEXT:\n${context}\n\nQUESTION: ${question}` }
    ];

    // 3. Call SAP AI Core BYOM (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
    const token    = await getAICoreToken();
    const deployId = process.env.AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID;
    const response = await axios.post(
      `${destination.url}/v2/inference/deployments/${deployId}/chat/completions`,
      { model: 'codesage-llama3', messages, max_tokens: 1024, temperature: 0.1 },
      { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`, 'AI-Resource-Group': 'default' } });

    const answer  = response.data.choices[0].message.content;
    const sources = chunks.map(c => ({ object_name: c.metadata.object,
                                       chunk_type:  c.metadata.type }));
    return { answer, sources, model: 'codesage-llama3', latency_ms: Date.now() - t0 };
  });
};

Prerequisites & How to Run

 Step 1 — Install developer tools (one-time)

# Verify Node.js after install
node --version    # Expected: v18.x.x+
npm  --version    # Expected: 9.x.x+

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
cds --version     # Expected: @sap/cds: 7.x.x

npm install -g mbt
mbt --version     # Expected: 1.2.x

# Install cf CLI from github.com/cloudfoundry/cli/releases (Windows .exe installer)
cf --version      # Expected: cf version 8.x.x

Step 2 — Install Node.js dependencies and test locally

cd C:\codesage\phase4_runtime\codesage-agent
npm install

# Set local env vars for testing
$env:CHROMADB_URL           = "http://localhost:8000"
$env:AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID  = "<your-deployment-id>"

cds watch
# [cds] - serving CodeSageService at http://localhost:4004/codesage

Test in a second PowerShell window while cds watch is running:

$body = @{ question = "How do we validate vendor payment terms?" } | ConvertTo-Json
Invoke-RestMethod -Method Post -Uri "http://localhost:4004/codesage/query" `
  -Headers @{ "Content-Type" = "application/json" } -Body $body
# Expected: answer + sources + latency_ms

Step 3 — Log in to BTP Cloud Foundry and create service instances

cf login -a https://api.cf.<your-region>.hana.ondemand.com
# Enter BTP email and password, select your org and space

cf create-service xsuaa        application  codesage-xsuaa        -c xs-security.json
cf create-service destination  lite         codesage-destination

cf services
# Both services should show: create succeeded

Step 4 — Build MTA archive and deploy to BTP

mbt build
# Creates: .\mta_archives\codesage-agent_1.0.0.mtar

cf deploy mta_archives\codesage-agent_1.0.0.mtar
# Takes 3-5 minutes. Ends with:
# Application "codesage-agent" started at:
# https://codesage-agent-<id>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Step 5 — Point deployed agent to on-premise ChromaDB

# Use SAP Cloud Connector to expose ChromaDB port to BTP, then:
cf set-env codesage-agent CHROMADB_URL           "https://virtual-chromadb-host:8000"
cf set-env codesage-agent AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID  "<your-deployment-id>"
cf restage codesage-agent

cf app codesage-agent
# Expected: requested state: started  instances: 1/1

Step 6 — End-to-end live test

# test_live.py  -- python test_live.py
import requests
token_resp = requests.post("<xsuaa-url>/oauth/token",
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
    auth=("<client-id>", "<client-secret>"))
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

response = requests.post(
    "https://codesage-agent-<id>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/codesage/query",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={"question": "Do we have a function module for vendor payment term validation?"})
data = response.json()
print("Answer:",   data["answer"][:200])
print("Sources:",  data["sources"])
print("Latency:", data["latency_ms"], "ms")
# Expected:
# Answer: Yes — Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS validates vendor payment terms against T052...
# Sources: [{'object_name': 'Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS', 'chunk_type': 'FUNCTION'}]
# Latency: 2841 ms

Step 7 — Register as a Fiori Launchpad tile (optional)

  1. In BTP Cockpit → HTML5 Applications, upload the ui/webapp folder.
  2. In SAP Fiori Launchpad Configuration Cockpit, add a new tile pointing to the HTML5 app URL.
  3. Assign the tile to a role collection mapped to CodeSage.User. Developers see the tile when they open Fiori Launchpad.

Phase 4 Outcome:  A developer types a question in plain English and receives a contextualised ABAP answer in 2–5 seconds, citing real function modules and programs by name. Every query is authenticated via BTP XSUAA, grounded by ChromaDB retrieval, and generated by the fine-tuned LLaMA-3 on SAP AI Core. No source code leaves the SAP BTP perimeter. Developers stop reinventing — they reuse.

Summary — what the developer gets: Every CodeSage answer is a synthesis of three layers: (1) what your organisation has already built — reuse, do not reinvent; (2) what SAP recommends as the correct standard approach; (3) what is safe and compatible for the S/4HANA journey ahead. No other tool in the SAP ecosystem combines all three automatically in a single developer query response.

entry.title: Introducing Our New Reply Progression Badges and a Color Refresh!
entry.content:
We’re announcing something new to celebrate the people who keep conversations going in the SAP Community. And we need your vote on a badge refresh.

Every month, thousands of discussions happen here. And they happen because someone, (maybe you?) took a few minutes to help out. Maybe it’s a quick tip you’ve learned the hard way, a workaround that saved your project, or just jumping into a tricky technical discussion to hash things out together.

Your contributions matter. They are what make this community what it is - a vibrant, collaborative space where everyone can learn and grow.

We wanted to find a new way to recognize your efforts so I’m thrilled to announce the roll out of Reply Progression Badges! To celebrate your contributions to the SAP Community, one reply at a time. The badges will launch Wednesday, April 22!

What Are Reply Progression Badges?
We’ve created a brand new badge set that celebrates your journey as an active community contributor. These badges are earned automatically when you reach specific thresholds of published replies on forum posts and discussions throughout the SAP Community.

Here are the milestones (replies given):

Replies given

How It Works - Earning the badges is simple:

  1. Find content that interests you – Browse across any topic area you’re interested in.
  2. Share your thoughts – Reply with your insights, experiences, questions, or feedback. Your perspective matters!
  3. Watch your progress – As your reply count grows, you’ll automatically unlock badges at each milestone.

Something I’m really excited to let you know, is that you don’t have to start from zero! These badges are awarded retroactively, which means all your past replies already count. If you’ve been an active community member and have already published 200 replies, you’ll automatically receive all the badges up to that milestone. Again, we'll launch the set on Wednesday, April 22. The launch will take place over several hours - since they are retroactive we don't want to overrun the system. Please be patient:)

To check your current reply count, go to your My Content page, and scroll down to the Private Statistics. You’ll find your reply counts there.

Once earned, your new badges will be on your My Content > Badges page. If you want to show them off, you can add your favorite badges to your content signature, so they are displayed next to your name on your posts and replies across the community. Check your My Content > Badges page today to see which badges you’ve already earned!

 

Replies Matter!
I’m really excited to launch this badge progression because every authentic contribution enriches our community. When you reply to a post, you’re doing more than just responding, you’re

  • Helping others solve problems
  • Building connections
  • Contributing to our collective expertise
  • Learning yourself 

Your experience might be exactly what someone needs. Great discussions and connections start with engaged replies. Future members will benefit from the insights you share today, and discussing concepts will help deepen your own understanding.

Over my years working on the community reputation program, I’ve seen first-hand how a single thoughtful reply can make someone’s day, or even their project outcome. The best replies are authentic. React to what resonates with you. Share what you genuinely know. Ask clarifying questions when you’re curious. Agree, respectfully disagree, or build on ideas.

One More Thing, Can You Help Us Add Some Color!?
Regarding our previous progression badges, we’ve heard your feedback loud and clear, they could use a refresh. Many of you mentioned that the badges all look too similar because they use the same background color. I have to agree now that I see them all on one badge profile.

 

So it’s time to make each badge set more distinctive and exciting! We’re planning to update the color on the following five progression badge sets that were launched last year:

  1. Blogs Published
  2. Discussions Published
  3. Likes Given
  4. Likes Received
  5. Answers Accepted as Solutions

Here’s where we need YOUR help!

We want each badge set to have its own unique color from the SAP brand color palette. The available color family options are:

Gray

Indigo

Teal

Green

Raspberry

Pink

Mango

Red

Since we have five badge sets to update, we’ll select the five most popular colors based on your votes.

How to vote: Simply reply to this blog post with your color preferences! List your top choices or just name your favorites. I’ll tally the votes from the comments and announce the winning colors in a follow-up post. I can’t wait to see which colors rise to the top, and I’ll be casting my own vote in the comments too!

In conclusion, the SAP Community thrives because of members like you who engage, share, and support each other. With our new Reply Progression Badges, we’re celebrating the power of conversation and recognizing those who make our community better, one reply at a time.

So act now, check your Private Statistics to see where you stand, claim your retroactive badges, and keep the conversation going! And don’t forget to give your color votes in the comments below. I can’t wait to see which colors win!

Have questions about the new badges or the color vote? Let me know in the comments. I’d love to hear from you!

entry.title: Two days of SAP community in Weinheim: CodeJam and Inside Track, 18-19 September 2026
entry.content:
Block your September calendar, we've got something for you. Two free community events back to back in Weinheim: a SAP CodeJam on Friday the 18th, the first SAP InsideTrack Weinheim on Saturday the 19th, and a relaxed get-together on Friday evening for anyone already in town.

Everything lives on sitwhm.de. Registration is open for both, and the Call for Speakers is open too.



Friday, September 18: SAP CodeJam with DJ Adams

DJ Adams (@qmacro) is doing a full-day CodeJam on CAP CDS modelling. The aim is to walk out actually understanding how CDS works, rather than the usual "I know the word but couldn't explain it under pressure" situation.

Here's the thing: even if CAP isn't your daily driver, a CodeJam with DJ is a great day out. Small group (up to 50 people), laptops open, everyone working through the same exercises together, DJ roaming the room to help when things go sideways. You get to ask the question you've been too embarrassed to ask in your team chat, and the person next to you probably has the same one. By lunch you've learned something new, gotten unstuck on two things you didn't realise you were stuck on, and made a couple of new friends in the room - a selfie with DJ can't hurt either. 😉 By 4 PM you're tired in the good way.

If you already work with CAP day to day, you'll close some real gaps. If you don't, you'll leave with enough foundation to try it on a side project, plus a much better feel for how modern SAP development hangs together.

Date: Friday, September 18, 2026
Time: 9:00 to 16:00
Location: Syntax Auditorium, Weinheim
Host: DJ Adams
Topic: CAP CDS modelling
Capacity: 50 seats
Event information: Hands on with CAP CDS
Registration: ti.to/sitwhm/codejam-whm

Quick heads up: the CodeJam has its own signup, separate from the InsideTrack. If you want to do both, register for both.

Personal note: I hosted CodeJams (in my time at SAP back in the days) myself for a while - together with DJ and on my own, and I loved every single one of them. It really didn't matter whether the participants were seasoned SAP developers or total beginners, they were fun for everyone in the room. The format just works.


Friday evening: Pre-event get-together

From 7 PM on Friday we're meeting up somewhere in Weinheim for food and drinks. Casual, no agenda, just people in town for the weekend hanging out. Speakers, organisers, early arrivals, whoever's around. Come join us. Food and drinks are on your own. The venue will go up on sitwhm.de closer to the date - once we have an indication of how many of you would join. 😉 There's a question on the ticket registration for SIT to indicate your interest, so please let us know!

Saturday, September 19: SAP InsideTrack Weinheim

For anyone who hasn't been to a SIT: it's a free, community-organised event with short talks (20 to 40 minutes), proper breaks, and no commercial agenda. Honestly, the best bits are often the conversations in between the sessions, and that's kind of the point.

The last SAP InsideTrack in the Rhine-Neckar region was in Walldorf, quite a few years back now. We've been missing that kind of day in the region, so we're making one happen. And we'd really like you to be there. 

Date: Saturday, September 19, 2026
Time: 9:00 to 18:00
Location: Syntax Auditorium, Höhnerweg 2-4, 69469 Weinheim
Capacity: 150 seats
Cost: Free
Registration: ti.to/sitwhm/sitwhm2026

The venue is a five minute walk from Weinheim train station, has free parking on site, and is wheelchair accessible. If you have specific accessibility requirements, drop us a line at team@sitwhm.de so we can set things up for you.

Why both on consecutive days

Pretty simple. If you're travelling in, a weekend is easier to justify than a single day. And the two formats do different things: the CodeJam is hands-on and focused on one topic, the InsideTrack is about hearing a bunch of different things and catching up with people. They go well together, but you're very welcome to come for just one of them too.

Call for Speakers
Got something to share? Please send it in on sessionize.com/sitwhm2026
Pretty much any SAP topic works. AI and Joule, BTP, ABAP and ABAP Cloud, CAP, UI5 and Fiori, integration, analytics, cloud architecture, clean core, migrations, security, developer experience, side projects, community stuff. If you're into it, chances are others will be too.

First-time speakers, we'd love to have you. Plenty of SIT regulars gave their first ever talk at a SIT. If you're not sure whether your topic fits, submit it anyway and we can talk it through.

Session length: 20 or 40 minutes
Language: English only
No product pitches

Registration and links:

CodeJam on Friday, September 18: ti.to/sitwhm/codejam-whm
InsideTrack on Saturday, September 19: ti.to/sitwhm/sitwhm2026
Call for Speakers: sessionize.com/sitwhm2026
Website: sitwhm.de
Questions: team@sitwhm.de

It really helps us if you register early, even if September still feels far away. It makes catering and space planning so much easier on our end.

Sponsors and helping out
We're still looking for sponsors, mostly for catering and a few practical things around the day. If that's something your company might be open to, get in touch at team@sitwhm.de or via LinkedIn. Same goes if you want to help on the day itself.

The organising team is Katharina Roeder, Julian Breitkopf, Dr. Dominik Schaefer, @matt_steiner and myself. Big thanks to Syntax for hosting us at the auditorium on both days.

Again: We'd really love to see you in Weinheim in September. Whether you come for one day, both days, or just the Friday evening catch-up, you're very welcome. And if you post about it, tag #sitWHM so we can follow along.

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: New SAP Champions, Vote Badges Colors and SAP Integration Suite highlights
entry.content:
April has been such a wonderful month, filled with news to be excited about!

From the latest updates in SAP Integration Suite, including the enhanced edition enabling agentic AI, to practical tips on integrating SAP Cloud Integration with SAP Translation Hub and insights to support SAP architecture decisions like CAP vs RAP, there is a lot to explore in this edition.

We also have exciting news from the SAP Community, including the announcement of new SAP Champions and a brand new set of Reply Progression Badges to recognize your engagement.

Have you attended any events from the Brazil CodeJam Roadshow? Today, we are also highlighting one of its biggest milestones: the 60th edition of SAP Inside Track in Brazil! Plus, don’t miss the opportunity to join upcoming community gatherings, including the New York City meetup and SAP Inside Track NRW in the Düsseldorf–Köln area.

Let's dive in!

Featured Blog Posts 

Agentic AI: SAP Integration Suite enhanced edition 

SAP expert Gautham Krishna introduces key information about the enhanced edition of SAP Integration Suite, highlighting the enabling agentic AI in enterprise environments, shifting the conversation from "AI that knows" to "AI that acts." For Gautham, the real challenge is not building AI models, but establishing trust through integration, governance, and secure execution across systems. And with new capabilities like Text-to-Flow, Script Optimization, API Anomaly Detection, and API Traffic Prediction, this enhanced edition helps both accelerate development and strengthen operational reliability. Combined with broader integration services and event-driven architecture, this release positions Integration Suite as the execution layer where AI agents can safely operate, scale, and deliver real business outcomes across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. 

Read Gautham blog post here 

SAP Cloud Integration tips for SAP Translation Hub 

In this practical blog post, SAP Champion Priyanka Chak demonstrates how to integrate SAP Cloud Integration with SAP Translation Hub, using a real-world scenario where error messages are returned in multiple languages, such as English and Japanese. The post walks through the full setup, including creating the Translation Hub service instance, configuring OAuth credentials, and designing an iFlow to connect with the document translation API. It also explains how different translation models can be used, including SAP's Neural Machine Translation and generative AI options for unsupported languages. With clear step-by-step guidance and testing examples, this is a useful prototype for developers looking to enable multilingual capabilities in their integration scenarios. 

Read Priyanka blog post here  

Q1 2026 Highlights for SAP Integration Suite 

Jumping back to SAP Integration Suite, SAP expert Maiara Ellwanger highlights the latest updates for Q1 2026, with a strong focus on improving operations and enabling smoother event-driven integration. As we mentioned previously, one of the key highlights is the introduction of the enhanced edition, but beyond the AI-driven operations and low-code tools, the update also brings improvements in event-driven integration, including better discovery and publishing of events in the Developer Hub and support for migration to Advanced Event Mesh. In addition, new features such as enhanced monitoring, message queue filtering, parameterized mappings, and expanded connectivity options-like customer-managed cloud connectors-aim to simplify troubleshooting and increase flexibility. With new adapters and expanded data center availability, this release delivers practical enhancements for integration of developers and operators working across complex landscapes. 

Read Maiara blog post here  

CAP vs RAP: Really depends? 

SAP Community member, Walter Aguero Machado published a blog post with one of the most common challenges in SAP architecture: deciding between CAP and RAP. The main problem is that for most situations, the answer is "It depends", which is technically correct but ultimately unhelpful in real scenarios. Instead, he shares the practical framework he uses with clients, addressing often overlooked factors such as how team composition influences architecture decisions, what to do with large volumes of legacy Z-objects, and why migrating from RAP to CAP is not a simple refactor. The post clearly explains where RAP genuinely fits, especially for tightly coupled S/4HANA extensions, and where CAP should be the default choice, particularly for multi-source, scalable, and cloud-native scenarios. He also emphasizes a key distinction: while both models can meet Clean Core requirements, CAP enforces it structurally, whereas RAP depends on discipline. This is a very honest and insightful read, and we highly recommend for anyone involved in SAP architecture decisions. 

Read Walter Aguero blog post here 

Community News 

New SAP Champions joining the program!  

Our SAP Community Team is more than happy to announce that we have welcomed 10 new members to the SAP Champions program (learn here more about the program)! Over the past weeks and months we met many outstanding SAP experts - some nominated themselves, others were nominated by peers - and it was inspiring to see the passion and generosity they all bring to the community. 

Choosing this cohort wasn’t easy: every candidate stood out for their consistently positive contributions. We selected experts from around the world who have been sharing knowledge through blog posts, helping others by answering questions in the platform, volunteering as event organizers, and creating impact across SAP Community and other platforms. 

Check out the new SAP Champions, you will soon learn more about them in the SAP Community Leaders Finder as well. 

Tonja Kehrer   

George Drakos  

Simone Azevedo  

Martin Jonen  

Dmitry Kuznetsov  

Yogananda Muthaiah  

Xavier Le Garrec  

Adarsh Rao  

Masashi Otsuka  

 

Please join us in congratulating our new SAP Champions! The entire SAP Community Team is excited to have you on board and we can’t wait to see what we’ll achieve together. 

We also want to extend a heartfelt thank you to everyone who nominated or was nominated for the program. Your enthusiasm and ongoing contributions - through blog posts, answers, events, mentorship, and more - make the SAP Community stronger every day. Please keep sharing your expertise and supporting others; we truly value every nomination and look forward to seeing more great contributions and nominations in the future. 

Introducing Our New Reply Progression Badges set and Vote for the new Colors! 

We have more news for our SAP Community users: new Reply Progression Badges and new colors for the badges! 

This brand new badge set celebrates your journey as an active community contributor. These badges are earned automatically when you reach specific thresholds of published replies throughout the SAP Community. 

How It Works? It is simple! 

  1. Find content that interests you – Browse across any topic area you’re interested in. 
  1. Share your thoughts – Reply with your insights, experiences, questions, or feedback. Your perspective matters! 
  1. Watch your progress – As your reply count grows, you’ll automatically unlock badges at each milestone. 

These badges are awarded retroactively, which means all your past replies already count. If you’ve been an active community member and have already published 200 replies, you’ll automatically receive all the badges up to that milestone.   

Now for the colors, we need your help!  

We have heard your feedback that the badges all look too similar because they use the same background color. So, the team has decided to update it and we want each badge set to have its own unique color from the SAP brand color palette. Since we have five badge sets to update, we will select the five most popular colors based on your votes.  

How to vote: You just need to comment your choices in the comments section of our SAP Community Admnistrator Caroleigh Deneen blog post here .  On her blog post you will also find more details about each one of the enhancements, and you can also reach out to Caroleigh for any additional questions.  

Events

Brasil CodeJam Roadshow: SIT Campinas Recap 

Last week happened the SAP Inside Track Campinas 2026, the 60th SIT edition in Brazil. Took place at FACAMP in Campinas and brought together an impressive lineup of speakers and community members. The event marked a special moment for the SAP ecosystem in Brazil, with the first visit of SAP Developer Advocates to the country, including @noravonthenen @thomas_jung @RichHeilman @Vitaliy-R and SAP Community managers @antonia_zorn @josh_bentley,. This visit was part of a nationwide SAP CodeJam Roadshow, which hosted more than 17 hands-on sessions across Brazil. The event featured a full-day agenda with sessions on topics like ABAP AI, Generative AI, SAP BTP, and Business Data Cloud, combined with a highly engaging and welcoming atmosphere that kept attendees connected and involved throughout the entire day. Thank you to all the event organizers, volunteers, and attendees. The Brazil community has been truly inspiring for all of us!  

Read Marssel recap blog post here 

New York City Meetup 

Jumping to North America, SAP Champion Sebastiano Marchesini has recently announced the plans for a New York City community meetup. With warmer days arriving after the long winter, Sebastiano is inviting members to share their preferences on date, time, and format - whether weekday evenings, friday happy hour, or weekend afternoons. Suggested locations include outdoor options like Central Park or spots in New Jersey, as well as rooftops, pubs, or coffee places. If you are in the area around the next couple of months, please make sure to comment your suggestions on his blog post and help collaborating for this great opportunity to connect with other members.  

Read Sebastiano announcement blog post here 

SAP Inside Track North Rhine-Westphalia  

In Germany, preparations are underway for SAP Inside Track NRW (North Rhine-Westphalia), taking place on June 13 in Dortmund at adesso, with a full-day agenda starting at 9:00 AM. The event is part of a two-day experience, beginning with an SAP CodeJam on June 12 at UNIORG, focused on "Latest ABAP Cloud and RAP Features", followed by an evening SAP Stammtisch at Hövels Brewery. The SAP Inside Track agenda features a strong lineup of speakers, including Andre Fischer, Daniel Schlachter, Karin Meyer, Sebastian Werner, and Martin Pankraz, covering topics such as RAP, CAP, AI, Fiori, and Integration Suite. And the event concludes with a networking afterparty as well. All the details are already published on the Düsseldorf-Köln local group and you can already register for the SAP Stammtisch, the SAP Inside Track, and the dinner via  the link: registration.  

Read Björn announcement blog post here

entry.title: Від ECC до S/4HANA: що важливо знати до 2027 року.
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA — один із найзатребуваніших напрямів в SAP-екосистемі. Багато компаній вже розпочали або планують перехід, проте для більшості фахівців та керівників залишається відкритим питання: що це насправді за система, чим вона принципово відрізняється від SAP ECC і що означає цей перехід на практиці. Щоб внести ясність, зібрали ключові моменти детального матеріалу — від базових понять до практичних аспектів міграції. 

 

Чому це актуально саме зараз? 

Перехід на SAP S/4HANA стає критично важливим, оскільки 2027 року закінчується основна підтримка SAP ERP (Business Suite). У 2027 році, компанії, що працюють на SAP ECC, більше не отримуватимуть оновлень безпеки, виправлень помилок, підтримки відповідності та аудиту, а також технічної підтримки від SAP.

 

Що таке SAP S/4HANA простими словами?

Це сучасна ERP-система на базі in-memory бази даних, яка об'єднує фінанси, HR, продажі, виробництво та логістику в одному місці.

Головна перевага — обробка та аналіз великих обсягів даних у реальному часі, що забезпечує значно вищу продуктивність порівняно з традиційними системами.

Доступна у двох варіантах: Cloud (публічний та приватний) та On-Premise.

 

Три причини мігрувати з SAP ECC до SAP S/4HANA:

  • Кібербезпека та відповідність вимогам (GDPR та ін.)
  • Несумісність із сучасними технологіями: AI, IoT, хмара
  • Конкурентне відставання без аналітики в реальному часі

👉Детальне порівняння

 

Можливості SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA допомагає приймати рішення швидше та працювати ефективніше. Ключові можливості:

  • Аналітика та звітність у реальному часі
  • AI/ML: прогнозування попиту, виявлення шахрайства, предиктивне обслуговування обладнання, аналіз клієнтів
  • Сучасний інтерфейс SAP Fiori
  • Автоматизація процесів та спрощена модель даних (звіти — за 2 секунди замість 30 хвилин)
  • Оптимізація ланцюга постачання
  • Хмарна масштабованість та інтеграція із зовнішніми системами

Різниця між SAP ECC та S/4HANA найкраще видна на конкретному прикладі — від фази отримання замовлення до фактичної оплати.

👉Детальна таблиця

 

Моделі впровадження SAP S/4HANA

Компанії можуть впровадити SAP S/4HANA по-різному — залежно від бізнес-потреб, рівня кастомізації та IT-стратегії.

Три основні варіанти:

  • Public Cloud (GROW)
  • Private Cloud (RISE)
  • On-Premise

👉Про моделі впровадження і різницю

 

Вартість SAP S/4HANA

Фіксованої ціни немає — вартість залежить від кількості користувачів, моделі впровадження (cloud/on-premise), необхідних модулів та складності проєкту.

Загалом хмарний варіант передбачає нижчу початкову вартість, інфраструктуру включену у підписку та швидше впровадження. On-Premise потребує більших початкових інвестицій і вищого TCO у довгостроковій перспективі.

👉Порівняння вартості Cloud vs On-Premise

 

Навчання та сертифікація

SAP S/4HANA потребує часу на вивчення, але опанувати її цілком реально. Залежно від обраного напряму, сертифікація займає від 2 тижнів до 2 місяців. Для старту доступні: SAP Young Professionals Program — навчання із сертифікацією та входом в SAP-екосистему, або самостійні курси на платформі SAP Learning.

 

Ознайомтеся з деталями у повній статті від Valentina Guevara та João Simões -  "5 Things You Need to Know Before Starting with SAP S/4HANA"

entry.title: SAP TALK Women 2025
entry.content:
No ultimo sábado, a comunidade SAP do RJ realizou o evento no Instituto Infnet, retornando a parceria.

Com temas variados, para quem já atua na área ou para quem quer começar, o conteúdo foi muito bom.

Lucyla Remzetti -> Desvendando as certificações SAP: Caminhos, Desafios e Oportunidades;
Fabiana Bacon ☁️ -> Entre o Hype e a Realidade: Os verdadeiros desafios da Inteligência Artificial;
Janaina Conde -> Qualificações relevantes para a carreira SAP com ferramentas para desenvolvimento de Soft e Hard skills;
Amarilys Urtubia ->PMO Protagonista: O Segredo das Mulheres no Sucesso de Projetos SAP.

Patricia Bahiense fez as honras do evento e das palestrantes.

entry.title: Erfolgreicher zweiter Stammtisch Basel - Freiburg / Edition Freiburg
entry.content:
Der zweite Stammtisch hat gestern stattgefunden - das erste Mal in Freiburg. Und, trotz starker Konkurrenz durch das Pokalhalbfinalspiel, war er gut besucht. 

In gemütlicher Atmosphäre haben wir uns kennengelernt und uns über SAP und Entwicklung und KI und vieles mehr ausgetauscht. Es war sehr schöner Abend, den wir gerne wiederholen werden - da waren sich alle einig. 

Der einzige Wermutstropfen war am Ende, später am Abend, die Niederlage des SC Freiburgs. Nachdem man das 0:1 noch bejubelt hatte...

Und aus unserer Sicht funktioniert das mit dem SAP Stammtisch auch multinational hervorragend. 

Bastian

entry.title: A recap of my amazing 4 stops in Brazil for the 3rd annual LATAM Roadshow 2026
entry.content:
As you may have seen we had an amazing 3rd SAP CodeJam LATAM roadshow organized for the third year by @ajmaradiaga and while I couldn't be there in March I made it down for not only some CodeJams but also an SAP Inside Track.  I got to meet with some amazing SAP Community Members, a few of our SAP Champions and some of my Community Relations colleagues.  Here is my stop by stop recap.  

Day 1 in #Brazil of my third annual LATAM roadshow for is completed (not counting travel days 😜 ) where I got to see some of the amazing sites in #Curitiba before heading to a meetup with a few colleagues, organizers, and an amazing sponsor: CHPro


The AI Core CodeJam with @thomas_jung and @RichHeilman  was sponsored by the amazing team at CHPro Solutions led by Calixto Herkert and our location host university Escola Politécnica PUCPR.
